result:
:::writing
At 3:17 a.m., the city felt like a held breath.

Not quiet—never quiet—but suspended, as if every sound had been asked to wait its turn. The refrigerator hummed in low devotion, a passing car whispered along wet asphalt, and somewhere upstairs a floorboard creaked like an old man remembering his knees. Mara sat at the kitchen table with a mug that had long since stopped steaming, tracing the crack along its side as though it might lead somewhere.

She hadn’t meant to stay up this late. That was the thing about late—no one ever meant it. It crept up, soft-footed, rearranging intentions until suddenly you were alone with your thoughts and the strange, intimate company of things that only existed at that hour. Regret. Possibility. The echo of conversations you wished you’d had differently.

The note was still on the counter.

She hadn’t touched it since she set it down. A single folded sheet, no envelope, her name written on the outside in handwriting she could have recognized from a mile away. Even now, years later, it carried the same careless certainty, the same tilt like it didn’t need permission to exist.

Mara.

Just that. No flourish, no apology.

She looked at it again and then away, as though it might look back.

It had been slipped under the door sometime between yesterday evening and this morning. She hadn’t heard it. Or maybe she had, in that way you hear something but decide it belongs to a dream. When she found it, she assumed it was a bill or a notice or something forgettable. Something that didn’t reach backward in time and pull.

But then she saw the name.

He wasn’t supposed to come back.

Not that there had been rules, exactly. No contract, no solemn agreement signed in ink and witnessed by the universe. Just the understanding that some endings, once reached, don’t circle around for a second pass. They dissolve. They become part of the sediment at the bottom of who you are.

Mara stood up and paced to the window. The streetlight flickered, indecisive, casting everything in a stuttering amber. Across the street, a cat slipped between shadows like it had somewhere better to be. She envied that certainty of motion.

“You’re being ridiculous,” she said aloud, her voice sounding foreign in the kitchen air. “It’s just a note.”

But the thing about just a note is that it never is.

She crossed back to the counter and picked it up before she could change her mind again. The paper was soft from handling—hers, not his. She had turned it over in her hands more times than she’d admit, feeling the weight of it as though it might reveal its contents through osmosis.

“Fine,” she muttered. “Fine.”

She unfolded it.

The handwriting inside was smaller, more deliberate, as if it had learned something in the years since she’d last seen it. Or maybe it was trying to be careful.

Mara,

I stood outside for ten minutes before I decided not to knock. That felt like something you’d hate, me just appearing like I had a right to be there. I don’t, I know that.

I don’t know if you’ll read this. I don’t know if you’ll recognize the handwriting or if you’ll throw it away before you get to this line. Both would be fair.

I’m not here to ask for anything.

(That’s a lie, but I’m trying to make it a small one.)

I wanted to tell you something I should have said a long time ago, but I kept convincing myself there would be a better moment. There wasn’t. There isn’t.

You were right.

There it was. Mara let out a short, humorless laugh that startled even her. Of all the things she had expected—apology, explanation, nostalgia—that was not one of them.

You were right.

It felt almost absurd in its simplicity. Like a key that didn’t seem important until you realized what it opened.

She read on.

About the way I left. About how I made it sound like staying would have been the mistake, like choosing you meant giving something up instead of gaining something I didn’t understand yet.

I told myself a lot of stories back then. About timing. About needing to figure myself out. About not wanting to drag you into something uncertain. They all sounded responsible, almost noble if you didn’t look too closely.

But the truth was simpler and worse.

I was afraid of how much I cared about you.

Mara closed her eyes, the words pressing against her in that quiet, insistent way that only truth manages. It wasn’t that she hadn’t known, somewhere deep and unarticulated. It was that hearing it—seeing it, fixed in ink—collapsed the distance she had carefully built around that knowledge.

Afraid.

She leaned back against the counter, gripping the edge of it as though the kitchen might tilt.

Outside, the sky was beginning to lighten in that hesitant way that precedes dawn. The held breath of the city was starting to release, one small exhale at a time.

She looked back at the letter.

I’m not asking you to forgive me. I don’t think forgiveness is something you can request like a favor. And I’m not asking to come back into your life the way I left it.

I just didn’t want the last thing between us to be silence that pretended it had all the answers.

If you want me to stay gone, I will. You won’t hear from me again.

But if you ever want to talk, I’m staying at the old place on Alder for a few days.

No expectations.

Just the truth, late as it is.

—L

Mara read the last line twice, as though it might shift if she looked at it differently. It didn’t. It stayed there, steady and unadorned.

No expectations.

She folded the letter carefully, aligning the edges in a way she hadn’t when she first opened it. The crack in her mug caught her eye again, a thin line running down the side, evidence of some small, forgotten impact. It still held liquid. It still served its purpose. But it would never be uncracked.

She wondered if people were like that.

Not broken beyond use. Just… altered in ways that couldn’t be undone.

The sky outside had moved from gray to something softer, a pale suggestion of blue. Morning was arriving whether she was ready for it or not.

Mara picked up her keys from the table, the metal cool against her palm. She stood there for a moment, feeling the weight of them, the quiet gravity of a decision that didn’t feel like a decision so much as a movement she had already begun.

“You’re being ridiculous,” she said again, but there was less conviction in it now.

Or maybe more honesty.

She slipped on her shoes, grabbed her jacket, and paused at the door. The apartment behind her looked the same as it always had—nothing rearranged, nothing missing. And yet it felt different, as if the presence of that folded piece of paper had shifted something fundamental in the air.

She turned the handle.

The hallway was dim, the light at the far end flickering in that same indecisive rhythm as the streetlight outside. For a second, she considered turning back, letting the moment pass, allowing the letter to become just another artifact of a life she used to have.

That would be easier.

It would also be a lie.

Mara stepped into the hallway and closed the door behind her with a soft click that sounded, in the quiet of the morning, almost like an answer.

At 3:17 a.m., the city had been a held breath.

Now, as she walked toward whatever came next, it finally exhaled.
:::

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
